phrasalVerbs.definition: To lock all the doors and windows of a building so that no one can get in.
examples: Make sure you lock up before you leave the house.
He locked up the shop at 6 p.m. every day.
Did you remember to lock up last night?

phrasalVerbs.definition: To start living in a different house or flat (with someone).
examples: They're moving in together next month.
We moved in last week and we're still unpacking.
She moved in with her boyfriend after six months.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To permanently leave the house or flat where you live or the place where you have your business.
examples: He moved out of his parents' house when he was eighteen.
The previous tenants moved out last month.
I need to move out by the end of the week.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To open a locked door, container, or building.
examples: The shop opens up at 9 a.m. every morning.
Can you open up the garage for me?
They opened up the old house after years of it being closed.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To let someone stay in your house.
examples: We can put you up for a few nights if you need.
My parents put me up while I was looking for a flat.
They offered to put up the guests for the wedding.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To get rid of something that you no longer want, for example by putting it in a dustbin.
examples: I need to throw out all these old magazines.
Don't throw out those boxes—I might need them!
She threw out her old clothes to make room for new ones.

fillInExercises.sentence: Don't forget to ___ ___ before you go on holiday.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Lock up' means to secure a building by locking doors and windows.

fillInExercises.sentence: They're planning to ___ in together next spring.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Move in' means to start living in a new place.
'Move out' means to leave the place where you live.

fillInExercises.sentence: Can you ___ up the shop while I finish counting the money?
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Open up' means to unlock and open a building.

fillInExercises.sentence: We can ___ you ___ for the weekend if you like.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Put up' means to let someone stay at your home.

fillInExercises.sentence: I need to ___ out all this old furniture.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Throw out' means to get rid of unwanted items.

fillInExercises.sentence: She ___ ___ of her apartment when she got a job abroad.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Move out' means to leave a residence permanently.

fillInExercises.sentence: Remember to ___ up the house before going to bed.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Lock up' means to secure the building.

lines.speaker: Sophie
lines.text: Are you excited to [move in (with)|move in (with)] to your new apartment?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: 'Move in' means to start living in a new place.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: *move in (with)* — To start living in a different house or flat (with someone).
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Are you excited to move in (with) to your new apartment?
explanationContent.commonMistake: Do not confuse this with other items from the lesson — here you need *move in (with)* (*move in (with)*).

wrongOptions.lock up: *lock up* (To lock all the doors and windows of a building so that no one can get in) does not match the meaning needed in this line.
wrongOptions.move out: *move out* (To permanently leave the house or flat where you live or the place where you have your business) does not match the meaning needed in this line.
wrongOptions.open up: *open up* (To open a locked door, container, or building) does not match the meaning needed in this line.
wrongOptions.put up: *put up* (To let someone stay in your house) does not match the meaning needed in this line.
wrongOptions.throw out: *throw out* (To get rid of something that you no longer want, for example by putting it in a dustbin) does not match the meaning needed in this line.

explanationContent.usefulTip: In conversation, *move in (with)* is often used in informal spoken English.
